runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 1 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Congressional Budget Office (2012). How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in the After-Tax Wage.
McClelland, R., & Mok, S. (2012). A Review of Recent Research on Labor Supply Elasticities.
runs.reasoningSummary: In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation models, such as those used by the CBO, primary earners typically have relatively low substitution elasticities. However, lower-income primary earners (such as those in decile 1) are generally assigned slightly higher elasticities than high-income primary earners, often around 0.25, to reflect greater responsiveness at the extensive margin (labor force participation).

runs.reasoningSummary: In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation models (such as those used by the CBO or PWBM), primary earners typically have lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. A standard substitution elasticity assumption for primary earners in the lowest decile is typically around 0.20 to 0.30.

runs.reasoningSummary: In standard U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation models, primary earners are generally assigned low substitution elasticities. runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit low substitution elasticities compared to secondary earners. However, individuals in the lowest earnings decile often face significant extensive margin decisions and interacting phase-ins/phase-outs of benefits (like the EITC), leading microsimulation models (such as those used by the CBO) to assign them slightly higher substitution elasticities (typically ranging from 0.1 to 0.3) than middle- and upper-income primary earners.
